Change his coat"


Intimate personal comment on
the conservation controversy was
scontained in letter read by
Smith. 1t was written by him to


Colonel H. c. Riser, acting director
Of the survey, dated August Is, and
was "a record Of the Pinchot-Bal-
linger trouble, which came to a
head at Spokane" when the irri-
cation congress was in session and
when Pinchot first publicly took
his stand against Ballinger. "The
whole conservation movement"
Smith wrote, SIS now linked up
with Mr. Pinchot's views on the
subject, so orthodoxy in the cause
means agreement with Mr.. Pin
chot. Ex-Governor Pardee, who
was down to speak on Irrigation
Interests of the People, spoke on
The Sins of Ballinger,' and though
he told me he intended nothing
personal and that he would be
glad to have me tell Secretary
Ballinger SO, he insinuated that
Ballinger was either a fool or
knave, and rather intimated the
latter as his favorite hypothesis"
